walkthrough.title: Place the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Sanctuary set up. First, unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. This is the shared board that holds the card display, the Break track, the Appeal track, the Conservation track, and the Reputation track. Find the Break token and place it on the starting space of the Break track that matches your player count. For a two-player game, look for the space marked with a 2.

walkthrough.title: Place the Bonus Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the 9 Bonus tiles. Shuffle them face down, then place one random Bonus tile face up on each of the 4 blank yellow Bonus spaces on the game board. Do not place anything on the two yellow spaces that already have icons printed on them. Put the remaining 5 Bonus tiles back in the box. They will not be used this game.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Zoo Card Deck
walkthrough.text: Now gather all 212 Zoo cards. These include 128 Animal cards, 64 Sponsor cards, and 20 Conservation Project cards. Shuffle them all together into one big face-down deck and place it on the game board on the space marked with the deck icon. Then deal 6 cards face down from the deck to the 6 display spaces on the board. These form the display, which you will reveal after everyone has chosen their starting hand.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the General Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the 2 organizers next to the game board as the general supply. Fill one organizer with the standard enclosures sorted by size: 28 one-space, 24 two-space, 15 three-space, 13 four-space, and 10 five-space enclosures. Fill the other organizer with money tokens, kiosk and pavilion tiles, special enclosure tiles, unique buildings, partner zoos, universities, X-tokens, Multiplier tokens, Venom tokens, and Constriction tokens. Keep some spare enclosures and kiosks in a bag as reserve.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Association Board
walkthrough.text: Place the Association board next to the game board, leaving room for a row of cards above and below it. Place 1 partner zoo from each continent and 1 university of each kind on the spaces provided on the Association board. In a 2-player game, take 6 player tokens of an unused color and cover specific spaces: on the Base Conservation Project cards, cover the left level on the left card, the middle level on the middle card, and the right level on the right card. On the Association board, cover the three spaces in the left column of the donation area. These blocked spaces cannot be used by either player.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out Base Conservation Projects
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the 12 Base Conservation Project cards and keep them face down next to the Association board. In a 2-player game, place the top 3 cards face up below the board. These base projects are available from the start of the game and never move or get replaced.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Final Scoring Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the 11 Final Scoring cards and place them face down next to the Association board. These will be dealt to players during personal setup and provide secret end-of-game scoring conditions.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Zoo Map
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a random Map A zoo map and places it with the Map A side face up. Map A is identical for all copies and is recommended for your first game because it offers generous rewards and an easy entry into the game. Return unused zoo maps to the box. For experienced players, you can use Map 0 for an intermediate experience, or the advanced Maps 1 through 8 on the reverse sides for unique special abilities.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Action Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one set of 5 Action cards: Build, Cards, Animals, Association, and Sponsors. Place each card with side I face up under the 5 card slots at the bottom of your zoo map. The Animals card always goes under the leftmost slot showing the number 1. Distribute the remaining 4 cards randomly under the other 4 slots. The slot number determines the strength of each action when you use it. Slot 1 is weakest, slot 5 is strongest.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Player Color
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color from the available options: black, blue, red, or yellow. Take all components of your chosen color. This includes 3 counters for the tracks, 4 association workers, and 25 player tokens.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Counters
walkthrough.text: Place your 3 counters on their starting positions. Your Appeal counter goes on the Appeal track based on turn order: the start player begins on space 0, the second player on space 1. Your Conservation counter goes on the first space of the Conservation track. Your Reputation counter goes on the first space of the Reputation track. Remember, the Appeal and Conservation tracks move toward each other. When they meet, the game ends.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Player Tokens and Workers
walkthrough.text: Take 7 player tokens and place them on the designated spaces along the left edge of your zoo map. These tokens get removed when you support conservation projects, unlocking bonuses along the way. Keep the remaining 18 player tokens next to your zoo map for later use. Take your 4 association workers and place 1 standing up on the notepad area of your zoo map as your active worker. Place the other 3 lying down on the designated spaces below the notepad.

walkthrough.title: Collect Starting Money
walkthrough.text: Take 25 money from the supply and place it on the notepad of your zoo map. This is your starting budget for building enclosures, playing animals, and other actions throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Starting Enclosure
walkthrough.text: On Map A, find the indicated space next to the kiosk. Place an empty size-3 standard enclosure there with the empty side up. This gives you an enclosure ready for an animal right from the start.

walkthrough.title: Draw Final Scoring Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player draws 2 Final Scoring cards and places them face down next to their zoo map. You may look at these at any time, but keep them hidden from other players. These cards provide bonus conservation points at the end of the game based on specific conditions. Later in the game, you will discard one of them, so you will only score from one card.

walkthrough.title: Draft Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Each player draws 8 Zoo cards from the deck. Look through them and choose 4 to keep as your starting hand. Discard the other 4 face up to the discard pile on the game board. For your first game, try to keep 1 or 2 Sponsor cards and 2 or 3 Animal cards. Make sure the cards you keep do not have conditions you cannot fulfill early in the game. If possible, choose cards with icons matching the Base Conservation Projects on display.

walkthrough.title: Reveal the Display and Begin
walkthrough.text: After all players have chosen their starting hand, flip the 6 cards on the display face up. Randomly determine a start player. Play proceeds clockwise. You are now ready to play Sanctuary.

summary.title: Place the Game Board
bullets: Place the game board centrally
Place Break token on starting space matching your player count

summary.title: Place the Bonus Tiles
bullets: Shuffle 9 Bonus tiles, place 1 face up on each of 4 blank yellow spaces
Return remaining 5 to the box

summary.title: Shuffle the Zoo Card Deck
bullets: Shuffle all 212 Zoo cards (128 Animal + 64 Sponsor + 20 Conservation Project)
Place face-down deck on the board, deal 6 face-down cards to the display

summary.title: Set Up the General Supply
bullets: Fill organizers with enclosures sorted by size (1-space through 5-space)
Fill second organizer with money, tiles, tokens, and other components

summary.title: Set Up the Association Board
bullets: Place Association board next to game board
Place 1 partner zoo per continent and 1 university per kind on the board
2-player game: block specific spaces with 6 tokens of unused color

summary.title: Lay Out Base Conservation Projects
bullets: Shuffle 12 Base Conservation Project cards, keep face down
2-player game: place top 3 face up below the Association board

summary.title: Choose a Zoo Map
bullets: Each player takes a random Map A zoo map (recommended for first game)
Map A is identical for all players — generous rewards and easy entry
Advanced maps (1-8) on reverse sides offer unique abilities

summary.title: Take Your Action Cards
bullets: Each player takes 5 Action cards: Build, Cards, Animals, Association, Sponsors
Place side I up under card slots (1-5) at bottom of zoo map
Animals card always starts in slot 1; others placed randomly

summary.title: Choose a Player Color
bullets: Pick a color (black, blue, red, or yellow)
Take 3 counters, 4 association workers, 25 player tokens

summary.title: Place Your Counters
bullets: Appeal counter on track: start player = space 0, second player = space 1
Conservation counter on first space of Conservation track
Reputation counter on first space of Reputation track

summary.title: Set Up Player Tokens and Workers
bullets: Place 7 player tokens on left edge of zoo map (conservation project bonuses)
Keep 18 tokens in reserve for later use
Place 1 active association worker on notepad, 3 inactive below

summary.title: Collect Starting Money
bullets: Take 25 money from supply, place on zoo map notepad

summary.title: Place Your Starting Enclosure
bullets: Map A: place an empty size-3 enclosure next to the kiosk on the indicated space

summary.title: Draw Final Scoring Cards
bullets: Draw 2 Final Scoring cards, keep them secret
One will be discarded later; the other scores 0-4 conservation points at end of game

summary.title: Draft Your Starting Hand
bullets: Draw 8 Zoo cards, keep 4, discard 4 face up
Aim for 1-2 Sponsors and 2-3 Animals with achievable conditions
Match icons to Base Conservation Projects if possible

summary.title: Reveal the Display and Begin
bullets: Flip all 6 display cards face up
Randomly determine start player, play clockwise

walkthrough.title: How the Game Flows
walkthrough.text: Sanctuary does not have formal rounds like most games. Instead, players simply take turns clockwise, one at a time, until the game ends. Between clusters of turns, there are Breaks triggered by the Break track. During your turn, you perform exactly one action using one of your 5 Action cards. The position of your Action card determines how powerful that action is. After your action, the card slides to slot 1 and everything else shifts right. That is the core loop: pick an action, do it, rearrange your cards, pass to the next player.

walkthrough.title: The 5 Action Cards
walkthrough.text: Your 5 Action cards sit in slots numbered 1 through 5 below your zoo map. The slot number equals the strength of that action. To take your turn, pick one Action card, slide it down, and perform its action at the strength of its current slot. After finishing, remove the card from its slot, shift all cards to its left one slot to the right, and place the used card into the now-empty slot 1. This means every action you perform drops to strength 1 afterward and slowly climbs back up as you use other actions. You can spend X-tokens to boost an action's strength by 1 per token, but strength above 5 rarely helps.

walkthrough.title: Action: Cards
walkthrough.text: The Cards action lets you gain new Zoo cards. First, advance the Break token 2 spaces on the Break track. Then either draw cards from the deck or snap. Drawing from the deck: check the table printed on your Cards action card to see how many cards you can draw at your current strength. You draw up to that many from the deck, then may have to discard one. Snapping: if your strength is at least 5, instead of drawing multiple cards, you may take exactly 1 card from the display regardless of your reputation. Replenish the display at the end of your turn. After upgrading this card to side II, you can draw from the display within your reputation range, and snapping drops to strength 3.

walkthrough.title: The Reputation Track and Display
walkthrough.text: The display holds 6 face-up cards on numbered folders. Your Reputation counter determines which cards are within your reputation range: you can access the card on your counter's folder and all cards on lower-numbered folders. Higher reputation means more cards are accessible. When you take cards from the display, do not refill it until your turn ends. Then shift remaining cards down to fill gaps and deal new cards from the deck to the top. You cannot look through the discard pile during the game.

walkthrough.title: Action: Build
walkthrough.text: The Build action lets you place buildings on your zoo map. On side I, you build exactly 1 building with a maximum size equal to your action strength. Available buildings are kiosks (size 1), pavilions (size 1), standard enclosures (sizes 1-5), and the Petting Zoo (special enclosure, size 3). Each building costs 2 money per space it covers. You must build adjacent to an existing building, or on a border space for your first building. Kiosks must be at least 3 spaces away from every other kiosk. After upgrading to side II, you unlock Reptile House and Large Bird Aviary (both size 5), can build multiple different buildings in one action, and can build on spaces with the special icon.

walkthrough.title: Action: Animals
walkthrough.text: The Animals action lets you play Animal cards from your hand. Check the table on the card for how many animals you can play at your current strength. For each animal, follow these steps in order: 1) Check conditions on the left side of the card, such as requiring specific partner zoos or animal category icons. 2) Pay the money cost shown in the upper-left corner. Partner zoos reduce cost by 3 money per matching continent icon. 3) Fill an empty enclosure of at least the required size, flipping it to its occupied side. Check for any water or rock space requirements. 4) Place the Animal card next to your zoo map. 5) Advance your Appeal counter by the amount shown on the card, plus any other bonuses like conservation or reputation points. Execute any immediate effects.

walkthrough.title: Special Enclosures
walkthrough.text: There are 3 types of special enclosures: Petting Zoo, Reptile House, and Large Bird Aviary. Your zoo can only have one of each. Petting Zoo animals can only go in the Petting Zoo, never in a standard enclosure. Reptiles and some birds can go in either a standard enclosure or their respective special enclosure. When you place an animal in a special enclosure, use player tokens from your supply to mark the spaces it occupies. When you build a Reptile House or Large Bird Aviary, you may immediately move qualifying animals from standard enclosures into the new special enclosure, freeing up those standard enclosures.

walkthrough.title: Action: Association
walkthrough.text: The Association action lets you send an active association worker to perform exactly one task on the Association board. The task's required strength must not exceed your action strength. The tasks are: Increase reputation by 2 (strength 2). Gain a partner zoo from a specific continent (strength 3). Gain a partner university (strength 4). Support a conservation project (strength 5). Each partner zoo reduces the cost of animals from that continent by 3 money per continent icon and provides continent icons for your zoo. Universities grant reputation and research icons. You can have a maximum of 4 partner zoos. To take a third or fourth, you must upgrade the Association action to side II.

walkthrough.title: Conservation Projects
walkthrough.text: Conservation projects are how you earn conservation points. Each project card shows 3 conditions at different levels, each with a corresponding reward. To support a project, fulfill one of its unclaimed conditions, then place a player token from the left side of your zoo map onto that condition. You earn the depicted conservation and reputation points as a reward. Removing the token from your zoo map also activates the bonus printed on that space. Some projects require releasing an animal into the wild: discard an animal from your zoo, lose its appeal, and free its enclosure. You can only support each project once.

walkthrough.title: Action: Sponsors
walkthrough.text: The Sponsors action lets you play exactly 1 Sponsor card from your hand, provided your action strength is at least equal to the card's level and you meet its conditions. Execute the card's instant effect and any triggered recurring effect, then place it next to your zoo map. Alternatively, instead of playing a Sponsor card, you can advance the Break token by the strength of the action and take that much money. After upgrading to side II, you can play multiple Sponsor cards in one action, and you can play Sponsor cards from the display within your reputation range.

walkthrough.title: The X-Token Action
walkthrough.text: If you cannot or do not want to perform any of your 5 actions, you can take the X-token action instead. Pick any Action card, move it to slot 1 as usual, and take 1 X-token. The slot's strength does not matter for this action. X-tokens are valuable because you can spend them to boost any action's strength by 1 per token. You can hold a maximum of 5 X-tokens at once.

walkthrough.title: Upgrading Action Cards
walkthrough.text: Each Action card has a side I and a more powerful side II. When you earn an upgrade, flip any one Action card of your choice to side II. It stays in its current slot. There are 4 ways to earn upgrades during the game: reaching certain spaces on the Conservation track, the Reputation track, and gaining your second partner zoo or second university. You cannot upgrade all 5 cards in a single game, so choose wisely which upgrades matter most for your strategy.

walkthrough.title: The Break
walkthrough.text: When the Break token reaches the last space of the Break track, a Break is triggered. The current player finishes their turn, then all players perform these steps in order: 1) Hand card limit: discard down to 3 cards (or 5 with a specific university). 2) Remove Multiplier, Venom, and Constriction tokens from Action cards. 3) Return all association workers from the Association board to your notepad. Replenish partner zoos and universities on the board. 4) Discard the bottom 2 display cards, shift remaining cards down, and refill the display. 5) Collect income: money from the Appeal track based on your position, money from kiosks based on adjacent buildings, and any income effects from Sponsor cards or activated zoo map bonuses. 6) Return the Break token to its starting space.

walkthrough.title: Appeal and Conservation Milestones
walkthrough.text: As you advance on the Conservation track, you hit milestones with one-time bonuses. At 2 conservation points, you either upgrade an Action card or activate an additional association worker. At 5 and 8 conservation points, you take either 5 money or one of the Bonus tokens next to that space. At 10 conservation points, the first player to reach this triggers all players to discard one of their two Final Scoring cards. If nobody reaches 10 before the game ends, everyone still discards one Final Scoring card before final scoring.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when a player's Appeal counter and Conservation counter are in the same scoring area, or pass each other. The scoring areas are spaces where one Conservation track position aligns with adjacent Appeal track positions. The triggering player finishes their turn, then every other player takes one final turn. After that, add any end-of-game effects from Sponsor cards and your remaining Final Scoring card. To calculate your Victory Points: find the lowest Appeal value in the scoring area where your Conservation counter sits. That is your target number. Subtract that target from your actual Appeal value. The result is your Victory Points. Highest positive total wins. Tiebreaker: most conservation projects supported.

summary.title: How the Game Flows
bullets: No formal rounds — players take turns clockwise until game ends
Breaks occur when the Break token reaches the end of the Break track
Each turn: perform 1 action, rearrange Action cards, pass to next player

summary.title: The 5 Action Cards
bullets: 5 Action cards in slots 1-5; slot number = action strength
Used card moves to slot 1, others shift right
Spend X-tokens to boost strength by 1 each (max useful strength: 5)

summary.title: Action: Cards
bullets: Advance Break token 2 spaces first
Draw cards from deck (check table for count) OR snap 1 card from display (strength 5+)
Side II: draw from display within reputation range; snap at strength 3+

summary.title: The Reputation Track and Display
bullets: 6-card display on numbered folders
Reputation counter determines which cards are in your range
Do not refill display until end of your turn; shift cards down, then deal new ones

summary.title: Action: Build
bullets: Build 1 building with max size = strength; cost = 2 money per space
Available: kiosk, pavilion, standard enclosures (1-5), Petting Zoo (3)
Must be adjacent to existing building; kiosks need 3-space gap between them
Side II: unlock Reptile House and Large Bird Aviary, build multiple different buildings

summary.title: Action: Animals
bullets: Play Animal cards from hand (check table for count at current strength)
Steps: check conditions, pay cost, fill empty enclosure, place card, gain appeal/effects
Partner zoos reduce cost by 3 money per matching continent icon

summary.title: Special Enclosures
bullets: 3 types: Petting Zoo, Reptile House, Large Bird Aviary (max 1 each)
Petting Zoo animals can ONLY go in the Petting Zoo
Reptiles and some birds can use standard OR special enclosures
When building Reptile House/Aviary, may move existing animals into it immediately

summary.title: Action: Association
bullets: Send 1 active worker to perform 1 task (must have sufficient strength)
Tasks: reputation +2 (str 2), partner zoo (str 3), university (str 4), conservation project (str 5)
Max 4 partner zoos; need upgraded Association for 3rd and 4th
Side II: perform multiple tasks, make donations for conservation points

summary.title: Conservation Projects
bullets: Fulfill a condition on the card, place player token from zoo map to claim reward
Reward = conservation points + sometimes reputation points
Removing token from zoo map activates that map bonus
Release projects: discard an animal, lose its appeal, free the enclosure

summary.title: Action: Sponsors
bullets: Play 1 Sponsor card (strength >= card level, meet conditions)
OR advance Break token by strength and take that much money
Side II: play multiple Sponsors, play from display within reputation range

summary.title: The X-Token Action
bullets: Pick any Action card, move to slot 1, take 1 X-token
X-tokens boost any action's strength by 1 each
Max 5 X-tokens in your supply

summary.title: Upgrading Action Cards
bullets: Flip any Action card from side I to side II for a stronger version
4 upgrade opportunities in the game (Conservation track, Reputation track, 2nd partner zoo, 2nd university)
Cannot upgrade all 5 — choose wisely

summary.title: The Break
bullets: Triggered when Break token reaches end of track
Steps: discard to hand limit (3/5), remove ability tokens, return workers, refresh display, collect income, reset Break token
Income = Appeal track money + kiosk income + recurring card/map incomes

summary.title: Appeal and Conservation Milestones
bullets: 2 CP: upgrade an Action card OR activate a worker
5/8 CP: take 5 money or a Bonus token
10 CP: all players discard 1 of their 2 Final Scoring cards

summary.title: End of Game and Final Scoring
bullets: Ends when Appeal and Conservation counters meet or cross
Triggering player finishes turn; all others take 1 final turn
Victory Points = your Appeal value minus target number (lowest appeal in your Conservation scoring area)
Tiebreaker: most conservation projects supported

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Practice
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with two players, which is the recommended player count for Sanctuary. Player 1 and Player 2 will take alternating turns so you can see how the action card system works, how to build enclosures, play animals, and start racing your Appeal and Conservation counters toward each other. Follow along move by move.

walkthrough.title: Starting Positions
walkthrough.text: Both players have their 5 Action cards in slots 1 through 5 with Animals in slot 1 (the weakest position). Each player has 25 money, 1 active association worker, and a starting hand of 4 cards. Player 1 starts on Appeal space 0, Player 2 on Appeal space 1. Both Conservation counters start on space 0. The display shows 6 face-up Zoo cards, and there are 3 Base Conservation Projects below the Association board.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Build an Enclosure
walkthrough.text: Player 1 looks at their hand and sees an Animal card that needs a size-2 enclosure. Good news: the Build card is in slot 4, giving strength 4. Player 1 slides the Build card down, pays 4 money for a 2-space enclosure, and places it on their zoo map adjacent to the starting 3-space enclosure. Then the Build card moves to slot 1, and the cards that were in slots 1-3 shift one slot right. Now Animals is in slot 2.

walkthrough.title: Why Building First Is Smart
walkthrough.text: You cannot play animals without empty enclosures. Building early gives you a place to put animals when the right cards come along. Notice that Player 1 also picked up a placement bonus by covering a bonus space on their zoo map. Those free bonuses add up over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Cards Action
walkthrough.text: Player 2 wants more options in hand. Their Cards action card is in slot 3, giving strength 3. First, Player 2 advances the Break token 2 spaces. Then they draw 2 cards from the deck. One is an Animal card with a cheap cost, the other is a Sponsor card. Player 2 keeps both without having to discard at this strength. The Cards action card moves to slot 1.

walkthrough.title: The Break Token Reminder
walkthrough.text: Every time anyone uses the Cards action, the Break token advances 2 spaces. This is easy to forget in the heat of the game. Breaks are important because they give you income based on your Appeal. Missing Break token advances can throw off the entire game pacing. So every time you see someone reach for their Cards action card, remind them: advance the Break token 2 steps first.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Play an Animal
walkthrough.text: Player 1 now has the Animals card in slot 2, giving strength 2. That lets them play 1 animal. They check the conditions on a Cheetah card in hand: no special conditions needed. The cost is 10 money and the Cheetah needs a size-3 enclosure. Player 1 pays 10 money, flips their starting 3-space enclosure to the occupied side, and places the Cheetah card next to their zoo map. The Cheetah gives 6 Appeal points, jumping Player 1 from space 0 to space 6 on the Appeal track. The card also has an ability that triggers immediately.

walkthrough.title: Why Animals Matter
walkthrough.text: Animals are the primary way you gain Appeal points, and Appeal is one half of the victory equation. But animals also provide category icons and continent icons that you need for conservation projects. Plus, many animals have powerful effects when played. The Cheetah just jumped Player 1 by 6 Appeal in one move. That is a huge swing.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Ignoring Enclosures
walkthrough.text: Player 2 is excited about the new Animal card they drew. It is cheap and has a great ability. They want to play it immediately, so they slide the Animals card down. But wait. Player 2 checks their zoo map and realizes they only have the one starting 3-space enclosure available. The animal they want to play needs a size-2 enclosure, and they could put it in the 3-space enclosure, but then they would waste 1 space of enclosure capacity on a size-2 animal. Worse, they have a bigger animal in hand that needs that 3-space enclosure later. Player 2 plays the small animal into the big enclosure anyway.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Enclosures
walkthrough.text: That was a costly mistake. Player 2 used their only size-3 enclosure on a size-2 animal. Now when they draw a bigger animal later, they will need to spend a whole turn building a new enclosure. In Sanctuary, enclosure space is precious. Always think ahead about what size enclosure each animal needs and try to match animals to appropriately-sized enclosures. Do not waste a big enclosure on a small animal if you can help it.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Association Action
walkthrough.text: Player 1 wants a partner zoo from Africa to reduce the cost of their next African animal. The Association card is in slot 5, strength 5 — more than enough for the partner zoo task, which requires strength 3. Player 1 places their active association worker on the partner zoo task, takes an Africa partner zoo tile from the Association board, and places it on the lowest empty partner zoo space on their zoo map. This immediately gives them 1 Africa icon and reduces the cost of all future African animals by 3 money per Africa icon on the card.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Association Action
walkthrough.text: Notice that Player 1 waited until their Association card was in a high slot. Since partner zoos require strength 3 and you might want to do conservation project work at strength 5 later, it is smart to use the Association action when it is in slot 4 or 5. Using it at strength 2 would only let you increase reputation, which is useful but less impactful early on.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Forgetting the Action Card Cycle
walkthrough.text: Player 2 wants to play a Sponsor card this turn. The Sponsor card in their hand has a level of 4, meaning they need the Sponsors action at strength 4 or higher. But their Sponsors card is sitting in slot 2 right now, giving only strength 2. Player 2 did not plan ahead. They burned their high-strength slots on lower-priority actions and now cannot play the Sponsor card they wanted. They are forced to take the X-token action instead, wasting a turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Manage Your Action Card Slots
walkthrough.text: The action card cycling system is the heart of Sanctuary. Every time you use a card, it drops to slot 1. Plan two or three turns ahead. If you know you need a high-strength Sponsors action soon, do not use the Sponsors card on a low-priority task. Let it climb to slot 4 or 5 first. Think of the card slots like a conveyor belt: cards enter at 1 and slowly rise. The key skill is timing your actions so the right card is in a high slot when you need it.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Sponsors Action
walkthrough.text: Player 1 plays a Sponsor card — a unique building that generates income during every Break. The Sponsors card is at strength 3, and the Sponsor card's level is 3. The building gets placed on the zoo map next to a rock space. Now every time a Break occurs, Player 1 will earn extra money from this building plus kiosk adjacency income. This is an engine-building move that pays dividends all game.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Recovery
walkthrough.text: Player 2 took the X-token action last turn, which was not ideal but gave them 1 X-token. Now their Sponsors card has risen to slot 3, and with the X-token boosting it to strength 4, they can finally play that level-4 Sponsor card. Player 2 executes it, gaining a useful recurring effect. The lesson: X-tokens are the safety net when your card timing goes wrong, but it is always better to plan ahead and not need them.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Forgetting Conservation
walkthrough.text: Several turns in, Player 2 has great Appeal — they are climbing the track nicely with animals. But they have been ignoring conservation projects entirely. Their Conservation counter is still near the start. Remember, the game ends when Appeal and Conservation counters meet. If your Conservation counter is too far behind, your final score will be terrible because the target number will be very low and your Appeal will not be high enough relative to it. You need both counters to advance and eventually cross for a positive score.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Appeal and Conservation
walkthrough.text: This is the most important strategic lesson in Sanctuary. You need to push both tracks. Appeal gives you income and eventually points. Conservation gives you milestones, bonuses, and determines your scoring area. A player who only builds Appeal will have a low or negative final score. A player who only pushes Conservation will not have enough Appeal to beat the target number. The best players keep both counters moving in tandem, then push hard at the end to cross them over each other for a positive score.

walkthrough.title: A Break Happens
walkthrough.text: After several rounds of play, the Break token reaches the end of the track. Time for a Break. Both players discard down to their hand limit of 3 cards. Player 1 retrieves their association worker from the board. The display refreshes: bottom 2 cards discarded, remaining cards shift down, new cards dealt from the deck. Then both players collect income: money from the Appeal track plus kiosk income plus any recurring incomes. Player 1 earns 15 money total thanks to their kiosk and Sponsor card income. Player 2 only earns 10 because they did not invest in income sources. The Break token returns to the start.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: The game continues with alternating turns. Both players keep building enclosures, playing animals and sponsors, taking cards, using the Association board, and supporting conservation projects. The Break track counts down again toward the next Break. Each Break brings income, so the richer your engine, the more money you earn each cycle. Player 1 is pulling ahead thanks to early engine investment. Player 2 is catching up by focusing on high-appeal animals and finally supporting some conservation projects.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen how the core action card system works, how to build enclosures and play animals, how association tasks and conservation projects drive your Conservation counter, and how the Break cycle provides income. You have also seen three common mistakes: wasting enclosure space, failing to plan action card timing, and neglecting conservation. Avoid those traps and you will be well on your way to building a successful zoo.

walkthrough.title: Before You Build Your Zoo
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to action cards, animal categories, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Welcome to Practice
bullets: Scripted opening with 2 players — the recommended count for Sanctuary
Follow along to see the action card system, building, and animal play
Covers the first several turns and a Break cycle

summary.title: Starting Positions
bullets: Both players: 5 Action cards, 25 money, 1 active worker, 4 hand cards
Player 1 starts Appeal 0, Player 2 starts Appeal 1
Display has 6 face-up cards; 3 Base Conservation Projects below Association board

summary.title: Player 1's Turn: Build an Enclosure
bullets: Build card in slot 4 = strength 4 — build a 2-space enclosure for 4 money
Place adjacent to starting enclosure, collect any placement bonus
Build card moves to slot 1, other cards shift right

summary.title: Why Building First Is Smart
bullets: No empty enclosure = no animals
Placement bonuses add up over the game
Build early to have enclosures ready when good animals appear

summary.title: Player 2's Turn: Cards Action
bullets: Cards card at strength 3 — draw 2 cards from the deck
Must advance Break token 2 spaces first
More cards in hand = more options for future turns

summary.title: The Break Token Reminder
bullets: Cards action always advances Break token 2 spaces — do not forget
Break pacing affects income timing and game length
Remind other players when they use the Cards action

summary.title: Player 1's Turn: Play an Animal
bullets: Animals at strength 2 = play 1 animal
Pay cost, fill enclosure (flip to occupied), place card, gain Appeal
Cheetah grants 6 Appeal — a huge jump early

summary.title: Why Animals Matter
bullets: Primary source of Appeal points
Provide category and continent icons needed for conservation projects
Many have powerful abilities when played

summary.title: Player 2's Mistake: Ignoring Enclosures
bullets: Player 2 puts a size-2 animal in their only size-3 enclosure
Wastes capacity — that 3-space enclosure could hold a bigger animal later
Now needs to build another enclosure, losing a turn

summary.title: Lesson: Plan Your Enclosures
bullets: Match animals to appropriately-sized enclosures
Do not waste a big enclosure on a small animal
Enclosure space is precious — plan ahead

summary.title: Player 1's Turn: Association Action
bullets: Association at strength 5 — gain an Africa partner zoo (requires strength 3)
Place partner zoo on zoo map, gain Africa icon
Future African animals cost 3 less per continent icon on the card

summary.title: Timing Your Association Action
bullets: Partner zoos need strength 3, conservation projects need strength 5
Use Association when it is in a high slot (4 or 5)
Low-strength Association only allows reputation increase

summary.title: Player 2's Mistake: Forgetting the Action Card Cycle
bullets: Player 2 needs Sponsors at strength 4 but it is in slot 2
Burned high slots on low-priority actions
Forced to take X-token action instead — wasted turn

summary.title: Lesson: Manage Your Action Card Slots
bullets: Plan 2-3 turns ahead — know what strength you need and when
Let high-priority cards climb to slot 4 or 5 before using them
Cards enter at 1 and rise like a conveyor belt — timing is everything

summary.title: Player 1's Turn: Sponsors Action
bullets: Play a unique building Sponsor card at strength 3
Building generates income every Break
Engine-building move that pays dividends all game

summary.title: Player 2's Turn: Recovery
bullets: Used X-token from last turn to boost Sponsors to strength 4
Finally plays the level-4 Sponsor card
X-tokens are a safety net, but planning ahead is better

summary.title: Player 2's Mistake: Forgetting Conservation
bullets: High Appeal but Conservation counter still near start
Game needs both counters to meet/cross for a positive score
Ignoring conservation leads to a terrible final score

summary.title: Lesson: Balance Appeal and Conservation
bullets: Push both tracks — Appeal for income, Conservation for milestones and scoring
Appeal-only or Conservation-only strategies lose
Best players advance both counters in tandem, then push to cross

summary.title: A Break Happens
bullets: Discard to hand limit, return workers, refresh display, collect income
Income = Appeal track + kiosk adjacency + recurring card effects
Break token resets to start — cycle begins again

summary.title: Continue Playing
bullets: Alternate turns: build, play animals/sponsors, draw cards, use Association
Each Break cycle brings income — invest in income sources early
Balance building your zoo with supporting conservation projects

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have seen all 5 core actions plus the X-token action
Three mistakes to avoid: wasting enclosure space, bad action card timing, ignoring conservation
You are ready to build your own zoo

summary.title: Before You Build Your Zoo
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for action cards, animal categories, and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the mistakes that trip up almost every new player. First, not building enough enclosures before trying to play animals. You cannot play an animal without a matching empty enclosure. Second, wasting big enclosures on small animals. A size-2 animal in a size-5 enclosure is terrible real estate management. Third, ignoring the Conservation track while piling up Appeal. Remember, your final score depends on both tracks meeting. Fourth, forgetting to advance the Break token when using the Cards action. This messes up game pacing for everyone. Fifth, letting your hand run dry by not drawing cards. You need a steady pipeline of good options.

walkthrough.title: Starting Hand Selection
walkthrough.text: During setup, you draw 8 cards and keep 4. This first decision shapes your opening turns. Look for animals with low costs that you can play early using your starting enclosures. Avoid keeping expensive animals you cannot afford for many turns. Try to keep at least one Sponsor card with an income or recurring effect for long-term value. Check the Base Conservation Projects on display and try to keep cards whose icons align with those projects. A balanced starting hand of 2 or 3 Animals plus 1 or 2 Sponsors usually works better than going all-in on one card type.

walkthrough.title: The Action Card Conveyor Belt
walkthrough.text: Understanding the action card slot system is the single most important skill in Sanctuary. Every time you use a card, it drops to slot 1 and slowly rises as you use other cards. The key insight is that you should plan two or three turns ahead. Before taking an action, ask yourself: after this card drops to slot 1, which card do I need in a high slot next? If your Animals card is in slot 5 and you have a great animal to play, do it. But if you need Build at a high strength in two turns, make sure you are not pushing Build back to slot 1 right before you need it.

walkthrough.title: Money Management
walkthrough.text: You start with 25 money and earn more during Breaks. Money pays for enclosures, animals, and other costs. A common mistake is spending all your money too quickly and then being unable to play animals when great cards appear. Try to keep at least 8 to 10 money in reserve after each major purchase. Partner zoos help enormously with money management because they reduce animal costs by 3 per matching continent icon. An Africa partner zoo paying for itself over just 3 or 4 African animals is common. Kiosk income also adds up, especially if you build them adjacent to occupied enclosures and other buildings.

walkthrough.title: The Dual-Track Mindset
walkthrough.text: Your final score is Appeal minus a target number that comes from your Conservation position. This means you need both tracks to advance. Think of it this way: Appeal is your offense, providing income and raw point potential. Conservation is your control, determining where your scoring area lands and unlocking milestones. In the early game, focus on Appeal to get income flowing. In the mid-game, start supporting conservation projects to push Conservation forward. In the late game, try to have both counters approaching the same scoring area from opposite sides. The moment they overlap or cross, you trigger the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Partner Zoos and Universities
walkthrough.text: Partner zoos are among the most powerful early investments. Each one reduces the cost of animals from that continent by 3 money per continent icon on the card. A partner zoo from Africa means every African animal with 2 Africa icons costs 6 less money. Over the course of the game, one partner zoo can save you 15 to 20 money. Universities are also valuable because they increase your reputation, grant research icons, and one of them raises your hand limit from 3 to 5. A higher hand limit gives you much more flexibility during Breaks. Try to get 2 partner zoos early and at least 1 university before the mid-game.

walkthrough.title: When to Upgrade Action Cards
walkthrough.text: You get 4 upgrade opportunities during the game but only 5 action cards. Some upgrades are more impactful than others. The Build upgrade is strong because it unlocks Reptile House and Large Bird Aviary, lets you build multiple buildings per action, and allows building on special icon spaces to fully cover your map for 7 bonus Appeal. The Animals upgrade lets you play animals from the display, which is extremely flexible. The Cards upgrade lets you draw from the display and lowers the snap threshold. The Association upgrade enables multiple tasks per action and donations. Prioritize the upgrades that match your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Break Track
walkthrough.text: Breaks are your income source, but they also force you to discard down to your hand limit. Time your Cards actions so you draw right after a Break when your hand is lean, not right before when you will just have to discard. Also, pay attention to how many spaces remain on the Break track. If the Break is about to trigger and you have income-generating kiosks and Sponsor cards, you are in great shape. If you are broke with no income sources, a Break just costs you time discarding cards. Use the Sponsors action to advance the Break token and take money when it benefits you, or hold off on Cards actions when you are near a Break and do not want to trigger it yet.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Build enclosures BEFORE trying to play animals
Match animal sizes to enclosure sizes — do not waste big enclosures on small animals
Advance both Appeal AND Conservation — ignoring either one ruins your score
Always advance Break token 2 spaces when using Cards action
Keep drawing cards — an empty hand means no options

summary.title: Starting Hand Selection
bullets: Keep 2-3 Animals with low costs you can play early
Keep 1-2 Sponsors with income or recurring effects
Match card icons to Base Conservation Projects on display
Avoid expensive animals you cannot afford for many turns

summary.title: The Action Card Conveyor Belt
bullets: Plan 2-3 turns ahead — know which card you need in a high slot next
Used card drops to slot 1 and slowly rises
Do not push a card to slot 1 right before you need it at high strength
X-tokens can boost strength, but planning is always better

summary.title: Money Management
bullets: Keep 8-10 money in reserve after major purchases
Partner zoos save 3 money per continent icon per animal — massive over time
Kiosk income adds up: 1 money per adjacent occupied enclosure/building
Income from Breaks is your lifeline — invest in income sources early

summary.title: The Dual-Track Mindset
bullets: Appeal = offense (income + raw points), Conservation = control (milestones + scoring area)
Early game: focus on Appeal for income
Mid game: start pushing Conservation with projects
Late game: get both counters to overlap/cross for a positive score

summary.title: Partner Zoos and Universities
bullets: Each partner zoo saves 3 money per continent icon per animal from that continent
One partner zoo can save 15-20 money over a full game
Universities grant reputation, research icons, and potentially higher hand limit (5)
Aim for 2 partner zoos and 1 university before mid-game

summary.title: When to Upgrade Action Cards
bullets: 4 upgrade opportunities, 5 cards — choose based on your strategy
Build upgrade: Reptile House/Aviary + multiple builds + full map coverage (7 Appeal)
Animals upgrade: play from display — extremely flexible
Cards/Association upgrades: display access and multi-task efficiency

summary.title: Watch the Break Track
bullets: Draw cards after a Break (lean hand), not right before
Breaks give income proportional to your Appeal and kiosk infrastructure
Use Sponsors action to advance Break token and take money strategically
Time your income engine investments before Breaks, not after

walkthrough.title: Appeal Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: One proven approach is to rush Appeal as fast as possible in the early game. The idea is to play high-Appeal animals quickly to climb the Appeal track, which increases your Break income dramatically. Higher income means more money per Break, which funds more animals. Focus on animals with 5 or more Appeal and low conditions. Build enclosures aggressively and play animals every chance you get. The risk is falling behind on Conservation, so pivot to conservation projects once your Appeal income is self-sustaining. A player earning 20 or more money per Break can afford to slow down and invest in Conservation.

walkthrough.title: Conservation Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The opposite approach focuses on conservation projects early. The Conservation track has powerful milestones: an Action card upgrade at 2, money or a Bonus token at 5 and 8, and the Final Scoring card discard at 10. By hitting these milestones early, you gain compounding advantages. The upgrade at 2 can unlock your Build or Animals side II, which dramatically increases your options. The Bonus tokens at 5 and 8 can provide massive one-time benefits. This strategy requires careful icon collection, so draft animals and sponsors whose icons match the available conservation projects.

walkthrough.title: Continent Specialization
walkthrough.text: Focusing on one or two continents is one of the strongest strategies in Sanctuary. Get partner zoos from your chosen continents early. Each partner zoo saves you 3 money per continent icon on every animal from that continent, and those discounts compound. An Africa partner zoo with an animal that has 2 Africa icons saves 6 money on one play. If you also collect Africa-focused conservation projects, your continent icons do double duty: they qualify you for conservation projects while your partner zoo keeps costs low. This strategy pairs well with the Conservation Engine approach.

walkthrough.title: Kiosk Income Network
walkthrough.text: Kiosks generate income during every Break based on adjacent buildings: 1 money per adjacent occupied standard enclosure, special enclosure (even empty), unique building, or pavilion. A well-placed kiosk surrounded by 4 or 5 buildings generates 4 to 5 money every Break. Two kiosks can generate 8 to 10 money per Break. Combined with Appeal income, this creates a money engine that lets you play expensive animals and Sponsor cards without worrying about running dry. The constraint is that kiosks must be at least 3 spaces apart, so plan their placement on your zoo map carefully during your first few Build actions.

walkthrough.title: Sponsor Card Synergies
walkthrough.text: Sponsor cards are often underrated by new players. The best Sponsors have recurring effects that trigger every time you play a specific animal category. For example, a card that gives you 2 money every time you play an herbivore turns every herbivore into a discounted play. Unique buildings placed on your zoo map provide income and abilities that last the entire game. End-of-game Sponsor effects, marked with a brown strip, can add significant Appeal or Conservation points in final scoring. Look for Sponsors that align with your animal category focus and conservation project goals.

walkthrough.title: Action Card Timing Mastery
walkthrough.text: Expert players manipulate the action card cycle to chain powerful turns. The key technique is setting up a sequence where you use your most important action at strength 5, followed by a secondary action at strength 4 or 5 on your next turn. Some animal abilities like Clever or Boost let you move an Action card to slot 1 or 5 after finishing your action, bypassing the normal cycle. The Fennec Fox, for instance, lets you place any Action card on slot 1 after your action, effectively letting you choose what sits in strength 5 next. Build your strategy around these ability combos for maximum efficiency.

walkthrough.title: Special Enclosure Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Reptile House and Large Bird Aviary each hold 5 spaces worth of animals, but they are shared enclosures. Multiple reptiles or birds can coexist in the same special enclosure as long as there are enough free spaces. This is hugely space-efficient on your zoo map. When you build one, you can immediately move existing qualifying animals from standard enclosures, freeing those standard enclosures for new animals. A mid-game Reptile House that frees up two standard enclosures is like getting two free Build actions. Time your Build upgrade and special enclosure construction to maximize freed enclosures.

walkthrough.title: Releasing Animals into the Wild
walkthrough.text: Conservation projects that require releasing an animal seem costly because you lose the animal's Appeal. However, the Conservation points and the freed enclosure can be worth more in the long run. The best release candidates are animals with low Appeal values that occupy large enclosures. Releasing a 2-Appeal animal from a 4-space enclosure gives you conservation points plus a big empty enclosure for a new, higher-Appeal animal. Time your releases for when you have a replacement animal ready in hand, so the freed enclosure does not sit empty long.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring Card Optimization
walkthrough.text: You start with 2 Final Scoring cards and must discard one when the first player hits 10 Conservation points, or before final scoring if nobody reaches 10. Each card awards 0 to 4 Conservation points based on specific conditions like number of Sponsor cards, animal categories, or icons. Since you know both cards from the start, let them guide your long-term strategy. If one card rewards Sponsor cards, play more Sponsors. If the other rewards diverse animal categories, diversify your animals. By mid-game, you should know which card you are keeping and optimize your remaining turns to maximize it.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Timing
walkthrough.text: Triggering the end of the game at the right moment is a high-level skill. You trigger the end when your Appeal and Conservation counters overlap or cross. After you trigger it, every other player gets exactly one more turn. If you trigger the end when your opponent is about to make a huge play, you deny them that opportunity. Conversely, if your opponent is close to triggering, you may want to accelerate your own scoring with a big animal or conservation project before they end the game. Watch both players' track positions carefully in the late game and calculate whether you benefit more from triggering the end now or waiting one more turn.

summary.title: Appeal Rush Strategy
bullets: Play high-Appeal animals early to climb the Appeal track fast
Higher Appeal = more income per Break = more money for future animals
Risk: falling behind on Conservation — pivot to projects once income is self-sustaining

summary.title: Conservation Engine Strategy
bullets: Hit Conservation milestones early for compounding advantages
2 CP = Action card upgrade, 5/8 CP = money or Bonus token
Draft animals and sponsors whose icons match conservation projects

summary.title: Continent Specialization
bullets: Focus on 1-2 continents with early partner zoos
Savings compound: 3 money per continent icon per animal
Continent icons double as conservation project qualifiers

summary.title: Kiosk Income Network
bullets: Well-placed kiosk = 4-5 money per Break from adjacent buildings
Two kiosks can generate 8-10 money per Break cycle
Plan kiosk placement early (3-space minimum gap between kiosks)

summary.title: Sponsor Card Synergies
bullets: Recurring effects (blue background) trigger on matching animal plays
Unique buildings provide permanent income and abilities
End-of-game effects (brown strip) can swing final scoring significantly

summary.title: Action Card Timing Mastery
bullets: Chain high-strength actions across consecutive turns
Animal abilities like Clever and Boost manipulate card positions
Set up sequences: strength 5 action followed by strength 4-5 next turn

summary.title: Special Enclosure Strategy
bullets: Reptile House and Large Bird Aviary hold multiple animals (5 spaces shared)
Moving animals into special enclosures frees standard enclosures
Mid-game special enclosure = free Build actions worth of enclosures

summary.title: Releasing Animals into the Wild
bullets: Best targets: low-Appeal animals in large enclosures
Conservation points + freed enclosure can outweigh lost Appeal
Have a replacement animal ready to fill the freed enclosure

summary.title: Final Scoring Card Optimization
bullets: Know both Final Scoring cards from the start — let them guide strategy
0-4 Conservation points per card at end of game
By mid-game, commit to one card and optimize remaining turns for it

summary.title: Endgame Timing
bullets: You trigger end when Appeal and Conservation counters overlap or cross
After triggering, each opponent gets exactly 1 more turn
Calculate whether triggering now or waiting benefits you more
Watch opponent track positions carefully in the late game

entries.definition: The 5 cards each player uses to perform actions: Build, Cards, Animals, Association, and Sponsors. Each sits in a slot numbered 1-5. The slot number equals the action's strength. After use, the card moves to slot 1 and all cards to its left shift right.
The number of the card slot where an Action card sits, ranging from 1 (weakest) to 5 (strongest). Determines how powerful the action is. Can be boosted by spending X-tokens (1 token = +1 strength).
Each Action card has two sides. Side I is the basic version used at the start. Side II is the upgraded version with more powerful effects. Upgrading flips the card in its current slot.

A token that can be spent to increase an action's strength by 1. Maximum of 5 held at once. Gained from the Break track, the X-token action, animal abilities, and other effects. Returned to the supply when spent.

An alternative action available when you cannot or do not want to perform any of the 5 standard actions. Pick any Action card, move it to slot 1 as normal, and take 1 X-token. Strength of the slot does not matter.

An administration phase triggered when the Break token reaches the end of the Break track. All players discard to hand limit, return workers, refresh the display, collect income, and reset the Break token. Not a formal round — just a pause in the action.

A token on the Break track that advances toward the end space. When it reaches the end, a Break is triggered. The Cards action always advances it 2 spaces. Some animal abilities and Sponsor effects also advance it.
A measure of your zoo's attractiveness. Tracked on the Appeal track on the game board. Gained primarily by playing animals. Determines your income during Breaks. One half of the victory equation.

The track on the game board where you mark your zoo's appeal. Higher appeal means more money during Breaks. Your Appeal counter and Conservation counter moving toward each other trigger the end of the game.

Points earned by supporting conservation projects and donations. Tracked on the Conservation track. Milestones at 2, 5, 8, and 10 provide bonuses. Your Conservation position determines your scoring area for final scoring.

The track on the game board for conservation points. Moves in the opposite direction from the Appeal track. Milestones: 2 CP = upgrade or worker, 5/8 CP = money or Bonus token, 10 CP = all players discard a Final Scoring card.

A measure of your zoo's prestige. Determines your reputation range in the display. Required to play certain Sponsor cards. Gained from association tasks, universities, and some animal/sponsor effects.

The track where you mark your zoo's reputation. Higher reputation gives you access to more cards in the display. Reaching space 10+ requires upgrading the Cards action. Space 15 is the maximum; excess reputation converts to Appeal.
The cards in the display accessible to you, determined by your Reputation counter. You can access the card on your counter's folder and all cards on lower-numbered folders.

A zone on the game board where one Conservation track space aligns with adjacent Appeal track spaces. Used for final scoring: the lowest Appeal value in your Conservation scoring area is your target number.
The lowest Appeal value in the scoring area where your Conservation counter sits at game end. Subtracted from your actual Appeal value to determine your Victory Points.
Your final score. Calculated as: your Appeal value minus your target number. A positive number is good. The highest positive total wins. Tiebreaker: most conservation projects supported.

Secret cards drawn during setup (2 per player, keep both until one is discarded). Award 0-4 conservation points at end of game based on specific conditions. One must be discarded when anyone reaches 10 conservation points.

Your personal player board representing your zoo. Contains the building area, card slots for Action cards, bonus spaces, partner zoo and university spaces, association worker spaces, and the notepad for money and workers.
The beginner zoo map recommended for first games. All copies are identical. Provides generous bonuses and an easy entry. Comes with a pre-placed kiosk and a starting size-3 enclosure space.
An intermediate zoo map with more bonus spaces. Suitable for players after their first game. All copies are identical. Found on the same physical tile as Map A.

Eight unique zoo maps on the reverse side of the standard maps. Each grants a different special ability. Recommended once familiar with the game. Players choose from player count +1 maps during setup.

The row of 6 face-up Zoo cards on the game board, placed on numbered folders. Cards can be taken via snapping, upgraded Cards action, upgraded Animals action, or upgraded Sponsors action. Refreshed during Breaks and at end of turns.
The area on your zoo map where you store money, active association workers, and X-tokens. All resources here are visible to other players.
The 212-card deck containing 3 types: Animal cards, Sponsor cards, and Conservation Project cards. All are shuffled together into one deck.

A Zoo card representing an animal for your zoo. Shows enclosure requirements, cost, conditions, category and continent icons, appeal value, and abilities. Requires an empty enclosure to play. 128 in the deck.

A Zoo card providing specialists, unique buildings, or special effects. Shows a level (minimum action strength needed), conditions, and effects (instant, recurring, income, or end-of-game). 64 in the deck.

A Zoo card representing a conservation project. Shows 3 conditions at different levels with corresponding conservation point rewards. Played to the Association board area. 20 in the deck plus 12 Base Conservation Project cards.
Conservation projects available from the start of the game, placed below the Association board. Never moved or replaced. 12 cards total; 3 are used in a 2-player game.

A tile placed on your zoo map to house animals. Comes in sizes 1 through 5. Has an empty side (yellow border) and an occupied side. Flipped to occupied when an animal is placed in it. Cost to build: 2 money per space.
A special enclosure (size 3) that exclusively houses Petting Zoo animals. Max 1 per zoo. Petting Zoo animals cannot go in standard enclosures. Both sides of the tile are identical.

A special enclosure (size 5) that houses reptiles. Requires the upgraded Build action to construct. Max 1 per zoo. Can hold multiple reptiles using player tokens to mark occupied spaces. When built, existing reptiles can be moved in immediately.

A special enclosure (size 5) that houses qualifying birds. Requires the upgraded Build action to construct. Max 1 per zoo. Works like the Reptile House but for birds with the aviary icon on their card.

A size-1 building that generates income during Breaks. Earns 1 money for each adjacent occupied standard enclosure, special enclosure (even empty), unique building, and pavilion. Must be at least 3 spaces from every other kiosk.
A size-1 building that immediately increases your zoo's appeal by 1 when built. On the reverse side of a kiosk tile. No ongoing effect after the initial appeal gain.

A special building placed via a Sponsor card, not the Build action. Each unique building has specific effects and may have placement requirements (adjacent to rock or water). 15 unique buildings in the game.
An icon printed on certain spaces of your zoo map. When you cover that space with a building, you immediately receive the depicted bonus (money, X-tokens, reputation, etc.).
A space on your zoo map marked with W. Cannot be built on. Some animals require their enclosure to be adjacent to water spaces.
A space on your zoo map marked with R. Cannot be built on. Some animals and unique buildings require adjacency to rock spaces.

A space on your zoo map that has a building on it. Covering all buildable spaces (excluding water and rock) grants 7 appeal. Some spaces with special icons cannot be covered until the Build action is upgraded.

A tile from the Association board placed on your zoo map. Provides 1 continent icon and reduces the cost of animals from that continent by 3 money per matching continent icon on the Animal card. Max 4 partner zoos; 3rd and 4th require upgraded Association action.
A tile from the Association board placed on your zoo map. Grants reputation increases and research icons. One specific university raises your hand card limit from 3 to 5. Max 3 universities.

A wooden piece used to perform tasks on the Association board. You start with 1 active worker and 3 inactive. Active workers stand on the notepad. Inactive workers are activated via Conservation track milestones. All workers return to your notepad during Breaks.

An optional part of the Association action (side II). Pay money to cover a donation space on the Association board and gain 1 conservation point. Costs increase as spaces fill up. Max 1 donation per Association action.

Taking exactly 1 card from the display regardless of reputation range, instead of drawing multiple cards from the deck. Requires Cards action at strength 5 (or strength 3 with upgraded Cards). Only 1 card is taken.

A type of conservation project condition. Discard a qualifying animal from your zoo, lose its appeal, and free its enclosure. The animal must have the specified icon and require a standard enclosure of the depicted size. Counts as supporting the conservation project.
One of the 7 animal categories. Some birds can be placed in the Large Bird Aviary special enclosure. Bird icons count toward conservation projects requiring bird icons.
One of the 7 animal categories. Herbivores are plant-eating animals. Their icon counts toward conservation projects and Sponsor card effects requiring herbivore icons.
One of the 7 animal categories. Predators are meat-eating animals. Their icon counts toward conservation projects and can trigger specific Sponsor card abilities.
One of the 7 animal categories. Primates include apes and monkeys. Their icon is relevant to conservation projects requiring primate icons.
One of the 7 animal categories. Reptiles can be placed in standard enclosures or the Reptile House special enclosure. Their icon counts toward reptile-specific conservation projects.
One of the 7 animal categories. A special subcategory with a black-on-white icon. Usually appears in combination with herbivore or predator. Counts as an animal category for all purposes.
One of the 7 animal categories. A special category with a black-on-white icon. Can ONLY be placed in the Petting Zoo special enclosure, never in a standard enclosure.

Icons representing the 5 continents: Africa, Americas, Asia, Australia, and Europe. Found on Animal cards, partner zoos, and some Sponsor cards. Used for conservation project conditions and to reduce animal costs via partner zoos.
Icons gained from universities and some Sponsor cards. Required for certain conservation projects and Sponsor card conditions. Tracked on the cards and tiles placed next to your zoo map.

Money and effects collected during every Break. Sources: Appeal track (based on position), kiosk adjacency, and recurring Sponsor card and zoo map bonus effects marked with the income icon (purple background).
The maximum number of cards you can hold after a Break. Default is 3 cards. A specific university raises it to 5. You must discard down to your hand limit at the start of every Break.
A token placed on an Action card that doubles its effect. Granted by certain animal abilities (e.g., White Stork's Multiplier: Build effect). Removed from Action cards during Breaks.
A token placed on opponents' Action cards by certain reptile abilities. Imposes a penalty when the affected action is used. Removed from Action cards during Breaks.
A token given to opponents by certain snake abilities (e.g., Anaconda). Creates a disadvantage for players ahead on specific tracks. Removed from Action cards during Breaks.
One of 9 tiles placed on 4 yellow spaces of the game board during setup. Provide various bonuses when triggered by specific conditions during the game.
An animal ability. After finishing the current action, you may place any Action card on slot 1. This lets you manipulate the action card cycle to set up powerful turns.

An animal ability (e.g., Boost: Animals). After finishing the current action, you may place the specified Action card on slot 1 or slot 5. Even more flexible than Clever for timing your next big action.
An animal ability. Draw a specified number of cards from the deck when played. Example: Sprint 3 = draw 3 cards. A powerful immediate effect for restocking your hand.
An animal ability. Reveal cards from the deck, choose 1 Animal card to add to your hand, and discard the rest. The number indicates how many cards to reveal. A targeted way to find specific animals.
An animal ability. Gain a specific card from the display when played. The number indicates the strength of the snap effect. Different from the Cards action snap.
An animal ability. Choose from options like discarding a card from the display and replenishing, or discarding from your hand to draw from the deck. Helps filter cards.
An animal ability. You may sell up to the specified number of cards from your hand for money each. A way to convert unwanted cards into cash.
An animal ability. Advance the Break token a specified number of spaces and gain that much money. Speeds up the Break cycle while providing cash.
An animal ability. Gain appeal or money for each matching icon in all zoos (or your zoo). Example: the Lion's Pack ability gains 1 appeal for each predator icon in your zoo.
An animal ability. Gain conservation points for each matching continent icon in all zoos. Encourages continent specialization and can award significant conservation points.
An animal ability. Place a card from your hand under this animal card to gain money. Converts unwanted cards into resources.
An animal ability. Place 1 free kiosk or pavilion when the animal is played. Saves you a Build action and its associated cost.
An animal ability associated with venomous snakes. Involves Venom tokens that affect opponents' Action cards.
An animal ability associated with constricting snakes. Each player ahead of you on the Appeal or Conservation track gains Constriction tokens, which impose penalties.
An animal ability. Shuffle the discard pile and draw cards from it, adding some to your hand and discarding the rest. A way to retrieve previously discarded cards.
An animal ability. Gain X-tokens when the animal is played. Provides immediate flexibility for boosting future actions.

An effect on an Animal card that can only apply if your zoo has 5 or more appeal. Marked with a special icon. The Appeal space marked on the Appeal track serves as a reminder. In solo play, these are replaced by solo-specific effects.
